summary: Gruber expresses unbridled excitement about Panic's Playdate, a handheld game player with a crank, calling it the most exciting product announcement since the original iPhone. He marvels that a small software company like Panic designed their own hardware and OS from scratch, in partnership with Teenage Engineering. The device features a black-and-white display, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and ships with 12 games for $149. Gruber argues Panic succeeds precisely because they're small and competing only on their own terms, leveraging modern Asian supply chains. He frames it as a throwback to the creative hardware era of the '80s and '90s, and closes with genuine personal pride for his friends at Panic.
key_points: Panic, a small software company, built a complete handheld gaming platform with custom hardware and OS — no Linux, no existing platform
Playdate succeeds by competing on its own terms rather than against state-of-the-art devices, with its only goal being fun
The $149 price point including 12 games is enabled by modern Asian manufacturing and cheap computing power
Gruber draws a parallel to the creative hardware startups of the '80s and '90s, but notes Panic isn't trying to be the next titan — which is the point
The product embodies the Alan Kay quote Jobs used: 'People who are really serious about software should make their own hardware'
money_quotes: The most amazing and exciting product announcement, for me, since the original iPhone.
Everything racing through your mind right now as 'but that's impossible' is, in fact, not impossible.
Panic is almost cheating in a way because they're tiny.

The Playdate platform isn't competing with the state of the art. It's not a retro platform, per se, but while it has an obviously nostalgic charm it is competing only on its own terms. Its only goal is to be fun.
In today's world all the new computing devices and platforms come from huge companies.
People who are really serious about software should make their own hardware.
I don't think I've spent a waking hour since when I haven't thought about Playdate at least once.
key_insight: Panic's Playdate succeeds as a hardware venture precisely because it's small enough to compete purely on charm and fun rather than specs, exploiting modern supply chains to revive the creative spirit of indie hardware from decades past.
